item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image came from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons, and the second and third images are from here and here respectively, and are both All Rights Reserved. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: I'm fine with the edit.

item.title: Wikiwalk Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded:
edit: edit made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show
- Hide

To link to another article featuring an anomalous GPS system, I propose the following edit:

Urban Wayfinding Unit, built by the ██████ corporation in 20██.

to

Urban Wayfinding Unit, built by the ██████ corporation in 20██.1

Footnotes
1. Non-conclusive evidence was later found linking the company's deceased founder to Elevix Electronics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GPS locator signals give test subjects' location upon entering SCP-1130-2 as [REDACTED]. This does not change until test subjects emerge from SCP-1130-2.

Really? Well, overall, is well written and interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you're absolutely right. That's how it works. :) Thanks for your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am up voting this and like it quite a bit. If I may make an observation, you detail the time the subject emerges without a time they went in. I feel like there is a sense that the subject was in transit a short time in the prime material plane, maybe the test started at 7 and they exited at 7:03 but had the effects of 2-4 weeks of absence. Is this correct? Or have I missed a detail? And if so please accept my apology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, my upvote also pushed this to 100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, how did I forget to upvote this? It reminds me of those older SCPs with the great exploration logs. We need more of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the praise!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea why I adore this article as much as I do. It hits home in a way few others have, and I love rereading it again and again.

item.title: Re: Transmitters
item.content:encoded:
No communication at all. The reasoning behind this is that subjects making use of SCP-1130 are not in this plane of existence anymore. Thus, anything attempting to send something directly to another place, be it a radiosignal, a GPS signal or any other kind of signal, will not reach its intended target, because it is simply not there. Audio and video can be used to gather information on the places it takes you, as long as you're not attempting to transmit it instantaneously but record it instead.

Oh wait. Read that wrong. No, they work, they just don't reach their intended targets. So if you're broadcasting a signal and taping it you'll end up with recorded data, but the signal won't be received in real time.

item.title: Transmitters
item.content:encoded: Do radio transmitters really not work at all while inside SCP-1130-2? Or is it just that their signal cannot be received outside? Also, AV recorders != radio transmitters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one had me at "It sounds like… like teeth…". The beginning was a little dry but the log is a winner, so +1. Interesting and well-executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trust me, I know the feel. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Go subject-verb. You're writing like a fiction writer. :)

Went subject-verb. Without directions no less.

And that's normal, I guess. For a fiction writer. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After skimming, it looks fine, though I could nitpick you on this:

Set at an angle into the front of the kiosk is a █████ brand LCD touchscreen

Go subject-verb. You're writing like a fiction writer. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What alternative would that be? The tentacled horror that whispers in the dark and magically makes you crazy?

All this before it puts you in its private zoo with all the other little mentally challenged macaques1 of course.

Footnotes
1. See SCP-1910's discussion page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: good. that makes it much better than the alternative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The directions actually give directions inside SCP-1130-2, but most people either freak out and forget to read them or lose them somewhere in SCP-1130-2. I guess that's not really all that clear.

Edit: … did it again.

Edit 2: …and AGAIN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so… do the printed directions include any explanation of what to do when you're inside, or do they just go "step through door, step out at destination?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No no no, tentacled horrors from beyond the veil should be able to get jobs. Just not from me.

That doesn't really sound any less awful, does it?

Edit: and thanks for the upvote, appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What, you don't want tentacled monsters to get work? Speciest!
(Upvoted, btw)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback, rewrote the log a bit to take out the more florid prose. Might have missed stuff though, so if you see something, give me a yell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I'm with you on that one. If I have to explain, I'm doing something wrong. Still mulling it over, but I think one of the things I need to do is make it clear that not everyone ends up a paranoid schizo. Some people are more mentally stable than others and spend less time in SCP-1130-2.

Fuck, I'm so used to sandboxing, I type SCP-XXXX-2 every goddamn time.

Anyway, thanks for the valuable feedback, I appreciate it. :)

Edit: I rewrote it a bit. The auditory phenomenon is still not at the top of the description, but it should be clear now that IF someone comes out all fucked-up in the brain department, it's not necessarily because of some magic insanity sound, but because they spent 4 weeks in there, drinking their own urine and eating toenails, constantly being pursued by a sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're a little too wishy-washy on making that aspect clear in the description. I had no idea that the chattering was the main culprit of the psychosis. You shouldn't wait to the end to reveal the hook, and I would revise the language to make it clear that the psychological state of the victims of the SCP is not directly attributed to an effect of the object.

Subjects exhibit highly erratic and aggressive behavior upon emergence from SCP-1130-2, necessitating appropriate measures to ensure maximum test efficiency.

This is what I'm talking about. You should include the words "psychological trauma" or something to that effect to prevent readers from thinking that the object drives people mad. Remember, it's not what you write, it's how it reads, and you shouldn't need to translate your article for someone to get the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only quibble with the log is that some of the description trends toward the florid ("pools of murky water", "lie here and there"). I liked it, though, and I like that this thing basically makes extradimensional spaces. A new twist on an old classic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd go crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, thanks for the upvote.

I instantly start disliking anything what-makes-people-crazy in order to have a fear factor. Whatever lies inside the SCP's effect should cause natural psychological damage, which is much more terrifying than the current idea. If the thing in the SCP made the D-class in the transcript remove all his teeth willingly (I think it was to placate whatever was following him in there.), I would definitely be scared of venturing inside.

Actually, the psychological trauma IS supposed to be a natural consequence of constantly hearing this chattering noise behind you. D-73299 began believing his own teeth were making the noise and thus tried to get rid of it by extracting them. I''m not sure why you'd think there is some magical crazymaking effect. These people are just locked inside a totally random dimension with some sound constantly grating on their nerves, right behind them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is good, reminds me of a lot of the older SCPs. There are things that could be improved on.

  • I like the idea of the door only appearing to those following the map, but I think it would be better if it appeared to everyone, but no one other than those following the map would be able to enter it. There are physics problems to deal with in terms of objects only appearing to certain people, and it looks quite odd when a person walks right through a wall.
  • I instantly start disliking anything what-makes-people-crazy in order to have a fear factor. Whatever lies inside the SCP's effect should cause natural psychological damage, which is much more terrifying than the current idea. If the thing in the SCP made the D-class in the transcript remove all his teeth willingly (I think it was to placate whatever was following him in there.), I would definitely be scared of venturing inside.
  • In regards to the transcript, it's main weakness at this point is that it slides between too many points of view. Tell the story from one viewpoint, whether it be the D Class audio log in journal form, a transcription of a video feed, or anything else you come up with. Check the logs for SCP-87 for examples on how to do one well.

I upvoted, but see if you can work on this stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, this is the first time it's the log that people find meh. Argh! Anything specific you feel bears mentioning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. The log is enh, but it didnt stop my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feel free to send me a PM when you have the time. I doubt I'll be in chat when you're done, but I look forward to your comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think you should get rid of it. While it's not a necessary addition to the article, you shouldn't remove it downright. It's not that it's bad, it's just unpolished. I recommend you get somebody to help you out on the dialogue, then see what people think.

I'd be more than willing to help you out, but I'm at work, so unless you're willing to wait…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: I am a stoopid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll add a line about it showing up in photographs. What I meant to say was that SCP-XXXX-1 is simply not there before you follow the directions. But I see that's worded a bit confusingly.

And another vote for shortening of the transcript. Hmmm…I removed a lot of the swearing too, so I wonder what to do with it. Get rid of it in its entirety? Are there parts you'd consider salvageable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You sly fox, you. Invalidating my point like that. *pout* Also, I call dibs on bass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's neat, although I do have a few problems. First of which:

SCP-1130-1 is not visible to those not making use of directions dispensed by SCP-1130.

I find it odd that it can be photographed and recorded, and then seen by those viewing said photograph or recording regardless as to whether or not they were following the directions. It just counteracts what you're going for in my mind.

On a second note, I'm not too crazy about the transcript, either. The dialogue seems scripted at best, and as Smapti mentioned, it has that "All D-Class Are Rugged Swearing Sailors" quality to it. It doesn't really flow or sound natural. Otherwise, I do like it, and I think the description of the locations are interesting, but the dialogue prevents me from really enjoying it.

Otherwise, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have edited my post to take into account the fact that it's not a disposal unit. And I now want to be in a band called CrazyMaker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *sigh*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the compliment. It's not a disposal unit of course, since the only things preventing someone from exiting is the amount of food or water they have on them, their ingenuity and the randomness in length of SCP-1130-2. I deliberately wanted to avoid having a portal where people just got chopped up, eaten or otherwise disposed of by some otherwordly tentacled horror. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'm a bit jaded to portals that D-class have unpleasant experiences in. I was liking the idea of the anomalous wayfinder, and then when it led you to the ACME D-class disposal unit crazymaker I was a bit disappointed. The quality of the writing prevents me from downvoting, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure I'm with you on the No-Good-Reason part, but I'll look into shortening it. Seems to me someone shoved through a door which promptly vanishes only to leave them stranded in what amounts to a floating deathtrap might have an inclination to use some bad language. Thanks for the upvote in any case. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article reminds me of a recurring dream i've had for years. Upvoting on the basis of that.

I don't really like the transcript part, though. It's long and pointless, filled with unnecessary and gratuituous amounts of what I call "D-class-Who-Swear-Every-Other-Word-For-No-Good-Reason-Syndrome", and it doesn't really add anything. Condense it into a brief description like one of the first few logs of SCP-093 and you'll improve this article considerably.

Overall, +1.
